z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024 extends federal aviation programs, funding authorities, and related taxes that were previously set to expire in March 2024. The Act primarily extends these provisions through May 10, 2024, or May 11, 2024, depending on the specific statute.

Under Title I, the Act authorizes specific funding levels for the Airport Improvement Program (AIP), Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) operations, air navigation facilities and equipment, and aviation research for the period of October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024. For the AIP, it authorizes over $2 billion and directs the FAA Administrator to calculate funding apportionments on an annualized basis as if $3.35 billion were available for fiscal year 2024, followed by a 39 percent reduction. The Act also extends the authority to obligate these funds through September 30, 2024. Additionally, it extends various miscellaneous aviation authorities, including aircraft insurance, unmanned aircraft systems testing, and small community air service programs, to May 10 or 11, 2024, and provides corresponding funding extensions for these programs.

Title II amends the Internal Revenue Code to extend the expenditure authority of the Airport and Airway Trust Fund through May 11, 2024. It also extends the collection of aviation fuel taxes, ticket taxes, and taxes related to fractional ownership programs that fund the trust fund through May 10 or 11, 2024.

Title III extends counter-unmanned aircraft systems (Counter-UAS) authorities under the Homeland Security Act of 2002 through May 11, 2024. 

The Act was approved on March 8, 2024, and serves as a short-term extension of existing aviation laws and revenue measures.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill extends the funding and legal authorities for several Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) programs and the aviation taxes that support them. It continues these programs from their previous expiration in early March 2024 through May 10, 2024, or in some cases May 11, 2024.

The legislation amends authorizations for the Airport Improvement Program, setting its funding at approximately $2.04 billion for the period from October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024. The bill directs the FAA to calculate funding apportionments for this period as if a full year’s total were $3.35 billion, then to reduce all state and grant apportionment amounts by 39 percent. These funds may be obligated through September 30, 2024. The law also provides specific funding amounts for FAA operations, air navigation facilities and equipment, and research and engineering, all for the same October-to-May period. Funding for essential air service and assistance for airports with insufficient service is similarly extended and increased to cover the longer timeframe.

A broad range of other aviation-related authorities are extended to the new May expiration dates. These include the FAA’s authority to provide aviation insurance, rules for unmanned aircraft test ranges and special unmanned aircraft systems, the competitive access reporting requirement, the Midway Island Airport provision, and various pilot programs and advisory committees, including those concerning aviation consumer protection, air travel for passengers with disabilities, and airport property redevelopment.

On the revenue side, the bill extends the authority to spend from the Airport and Airway Trust Fund and extends the aviation excise taxes that finance the fund. This includes taxes on aviation fuel, passenger tickets, and provisions related to fractional ownership programs. Additionally, the legislation extends the Department of Homeland Security’s counter-unmanned aircraft systems (Counter-UAS) authorities.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024
This act temporarily extends specific Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) programs and activities through May 10, 2024.
TITLE I--FEDERAL AVIATION PROGRAMS
(Sec. 101) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the Airport Improvement Program (AIP). This program provides grants for planning, development, and noise compatibility projects at or associated with certain public-use airports.
(Sec. 102) This section extends through May 10, 2024, certain expiring Department of Transportation (DOT) and FAA authorities and programs.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of DOT to provide insurance and reinsurance for the federal government for risks from operating an aircraft and providing related goods or services.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) Test Site Program, which provides verification of the safety of public and civil UAS, operations, and related navigation procedures before their integration into the national airspace system.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of DOT to provide exemptions from some requirements for UAS by using a risk-based approach to determine if certain UAS may operate safely in the national airspace system.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's airport safety and airspace hazard mitigation and enforcement program to test and evaluate technologies or systems that detect and mitigate potential aviation safety risks posed by UAS.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, competitive access disclosure requirements for AIP grant applications for large and medium hub airports. Specifically, an airport must provide assurances to DOT that it will report if it has been unable to accommodate one or more requests by an air carrier for access to gates or other facilities at that airport in order to provide service or expanded service.
This section extends authorization through May 10, 2024, for AIP grants for airports located in the Republic of the Marshall Islands, the Federated States of Micronesia, and the Republic of Palau.
This section extends authorization through May 10, 2024, for the FAA AIP supplementary discretionary funds for grants for airport or terminal development projects.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, AIP grant authorization for compatible land use planning and projects by state and local governments. Airport-compatible land uses are those that can coexist with a nearby airport without constraining the safe and efficient operation of the airport, or exposing people living or working nearby to significant environmental impacts.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authorization for the AIP non-movement area surveillance surface display systems pilot grant program. These systems use on-airport sensors to track vehicles or aircraft that are equipped with transponders on portions of the airfield surface that are not under the control of air traffic control.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authorization for the FAA weather reporting programs.
This section extends to May 11, 2024, a learning period (i.e., moratorium) that applies to certain DOT commercial spaceflight safety regulations governing the design or operation of a launch vehicle to protect the health and safety of crew, government astronauts, and space flight participants.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authority for DOT to enter into a reimbursable agreement with the Department of Interior for the purpose of funding airport development at Midway Island Airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the termination date for a final order establishing mileage and adjustment eligibility under the Essential Air Service (EAS) Program using the most commonly used route between the place and the nearest medium hub airport or large hub airport. (The EAS program was established to ensure that small communities that were served by certificated air carriers before the Airline Deregulation Act of 1978 would continue to receive scheduled passenger service.)
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the moratorium on discontinuing the FAA Contract Weather Observer Program at any airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA remote tower pilot program. A remote tower is a remotely operated air navigation facility, including all necessary system components, that provides the functions and capabilities of an air traffic control tower whereby air traffic services are provided to operators at an airport from a location that may not be on or near the airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for DOT to provide AIP grants for airport access roads in certain remote locations and for storage facilities for certain snow removal and safety equipment.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA's UAS remote detection and identification pilot program. Under the program, the FAA uses available remote detection or identification technologies for safety oversight, including enforcement actions against operators of UAS that are not in compliance with federal aviation laws and regulations.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the Aviation Consumer Protection Advisory Committee, which advises DOT in carrying out activities relating to airline customer service improvements.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the requirement that the Aviation Consumer Advocate at DOT submit an annual report to Congress summarizing aviation consumer complaints and recommendations.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA Air Carrier Access Act Advisory Committee to address the needs of passengers with disabilities.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for an FAA pilot program to provide air traffic control services on a preferential basis to aircraft equipped with certain Next Generation Air Transportation System (NextGen) avionics.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the pilot program that allows AIP funds to be used for activities related to the redevelopment of airport properties.
(Sec. 103) This section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, FAA salaries, operations, and maintenance.
Further, this section extends through May 10, 2024, DOT's authority to reduce the FAA's expenses for non-safety-related activities if DOT determines that the funding levels are insufficient to meet the FAA's authorized salary, operations, and maintenance expenses.
(Sec. 104) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority to acquire, establish, and improve air navigation facilities and equipment.
(Sec. 105) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority to conduct civil aviation research, engineering, and development.
(Sec. 106) This section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, the FAA Essential Air Service Program.
Further, this section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, the Small Community Air Service Development Program, a DOT grant program designed to help small communities address air service and airfare issues.
TITLE II--AVIATION REVENUE PROVISIONS
(Sec. 201) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority for expenditures from the Airport and Airway Trust Fund (AATF). The AATF is the primary funding source for all major FAA accounts that fund federal aviation programs, with the remainder coming from general fund appropriations.
(Sec. 202) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA to collect various taxes and fees to fund the AATF, including taxes on aviation fuel and airline tickets.
TITLE III--MISCELLANEOUS EXTENSIONS
(Sec. 301) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of the Department of Homeland Security and the Department of Justice to take certain actions to mitigate a credible threat to certain facilities or assets from an unmanned aircraft system (UAS). These include certain facilities that are located in the United States and identified as high-risk and a potential target for unlawful UAS activity.

bills.title: Veterans’ Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Veterans' Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-130) directs the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to increase the rates of certain benefits paid to veterans with service-connected disabilities and to survivors of certain disabled veterans, effective December 1, 2024.

The bill requires the Secretary to raise the dollar amounts that were in effect on November 30, 2024, for the following benefits:
- Wartime disability compensation (under section 1114 of title 38, U.S. Code);
- Additional compensation for dependents (section 1115(1));
- The clothing allowance (section 1162);
- Dependency and indemnity compensation paid to surviving spouses (section 1311, subsections (a) through (d)); and
- Dependency and indemnity compensation paid to children (sections 1313(a) and 1314).

The size of the increase is tied to the cost-of-living adjustment applied to Social Security benefits. Specifically, each covered amount is to be increased by the same percentage as the increase in Social Security benefits under title II of the Social Security Act that takes effect December 1, 2024, as determined under section 215(i) of that Act.

The Act also permits the Secretary to administratively adjust, consistent with these increases, the disability compensation rates payable to certain persons under section 10 of Public Law 85-857 who have not received compensation under chapter 11 of title 38.

The Secretary is required to publish the adjusted amounts in the Federal Register no later than the date by which the corresponding Social Security cost-of-living figures must be published for fiscal year 2025.

The law does not specify a fixed percentage increase itself; it links the increase to the Social Security adjustment. It was approved November 25, 2024.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Veterans’ Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024 requires the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to increase certain veterans’ disability compensation and survivor benefit payments, effective December 1, 2024, to match the annual cost‑of‑living adjustment made for Social Security benefits under title II of the Social Security Act.

The bill directs the Secretary to raise the dollar amounts in effect on November 30, 2024, for the following categories of benefits: wartime disability compensation (38 U.S.C. § 1114), additional compensation for dependents (38 U.S.C. § 1115(1)), the clothing allowance (38 U.S.C. § 1162), dependency and indemnity compensation for surviving spouses (38 U.S.C. § 1311(a)–(d)), and dependency and indemnity compensation for children (38 U.S.C. §§ 1313(a) and 1314). Each increase is set at the same percentage by which benefits under title II of the Social Security Act are increased effective December 1, 2024, as determined under section 215(i) of that Act.

The bill also contains a special rule permitting the Secretary to make corresponding administrative adjustments to disability compensation payable under section 10 of Public Law 85‑857 for certain persons who are not receiving compensation under chapter 11 of title 38.

To provide transparency, the Secretary must publish the adjusted dollar amounts in the Federal Register no later than the date on which the Social Security Administration is required to publish the related COLA determination under section 215(i)(2)(D) of the Social Security Act during fiscal year 2025. No other changes to eligibility or benefit structure are made; the law solely implements an automatic annual cost‑of‑living increase consistent with Social Security adjustments.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The bill, titled the “Police Our Border Act,” would require the Attorney General to submit a report to Congress on the impact of what the bill refers to as the “Biden border crisis” on law enforcement officers at the federal, state, local, and Tribal levels.

The report would be due no later than 180 days after enactment. It would have to address several topics, including the estimated dollar amount of resources devoted to addressing the border situation and the extent to which those resources are unavailable to law enforcement agencies; law enforcement officers’ exposure to fentanyl resulting from encounters with “illegal aliens” at the border and within the United States, including the nationality of each such person; injuries to law enforcement officers connected to the border situation or fentanyl exposure; officer morale; and the effect on recruiting and hiring law enforcement personnel.

The report would also have to estimate how much states have spent on local law enforcement efforts to investigate, intercept, and process fentanyl trafficked from the U.S. border, including the full evidence lifecycle from collection and analysis through storage and final destruction. It would also have to state how many law enforcement officers have been reassigned and how many local resources have been reallocated for those purposes.

The bill does not amend existing law. Its findings state Congress’s views on law enforcement, border security, fentanyl, and the President’s authority under section 212(f) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, but the operative provision is the reporting requirement.

The Police Our Border Act requires the Attorney General to submit a report to Congress detailing the impact of the border crisis on Federal, State, local, and Tribal law enforcement officers. No later than 180 days after the date of enactment, the Attorney General must provide a report that addresses several specific areas. 

The report must include the estimated dollar amount of resources devoted to addressing the border crisis that are consequently unavailable to law enforcement agencies. It must also cover the exposure of law enforcement officers to fentanyl resulting from encounters with illegal aliens at the border and within the United States, including the nationality of each such alien, as well as any injuries to officers connected to the crisis or fentanyl exposure. 

Additionally, the report must assess the morale of law enforcement officers and the impact of the border crisis on law enforcement recruiting and hiring. It must provide the estimated dollar amount that states have expended on local law enforcement efforts to investigate, intercept, and process fentanyl trafficked from the United States border, covering the entire evidence lifecycle from collection and analysis to storage and final destruction. Finally, the report must state how many law enforcement officers have been reassigned and how many local resources have been reallocated for these fentanyl-related efforts. The Act also includes congressional findings regarding the strain on law enforcement resources and officer safety due to the border crisis.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, titled the “Police Our Border Act,” requires the Attorney General to submit a report to Congress on how the situation at the southwest border is affecting law enforcement at the federal, state, local, and tribal levels. The report is due no later than 180 days after the bill becomes law.

The report must cover seven specific areas: (1) the estimated dollar amount of resources devoted to addressing the border situation and the extent to which those resources are unavailable to law enforcement agencies; (2) law enforcement officers’ exposure to fentanyl resulting from encounters with noncitizens at the border and inside the United States, including the nationality of each such noncitizen; (3) injuries to law enforcement officers connected to the border situation or fentanyl exposure; (4) officer morale; (5) the impact on recruiting and hiring law enforcement personnel; (6) the estimated dollar amount states have spent on local law enforcement efforts to investigate, intercept, and process fentanyl trafficked from the U.S. border, covering the entire evidence lifecycle from collection and analysis through storage and final destruction; and (7) the number of law enforcement officers reassigned and local resources reallocated for those same fentanyl-related activities.

The bill includes a series of congressional findings that describe the border situation as a crisis, attribute it to the current administration, and state that it has burdened law enforcement agencies, exposed officers to fentanyl, and made every state a border state. The findings also express support for passing border security legislation and for the President to use existing authorities to secure the southern border. The bill does not itself enact any new border security measures, authorize funding, or change immigration law; it solely mandates the production of the described report.

bills.title: Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act (Public Law 118-185) directs the Secretary of the Interior, acting through the Commissioner of Reclamation, to transfer certain federal land at Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir in Nebraska to Hitchcock County and Frontier County.

The land involved consists of five parcels: permitted cabin lots and concession lands at each reservoir, plus concession land at Swanson, totaling roughly 77 acres. Within three years of enactment, the Secretary must make good-faith efforts to negotiate and enter into "title transfer agreements" with each county to convey all federal interest in the applicable parcels. The agreements must require that each parcel be conveyed in whole, not subdivided, and that the receiving county demonstrate the technical and financial capacity to operate and maintain the land and bring it into compliance with state fire, safety, and health codes within two years of conveyance.

As consideration, each county must pay the fair market value of the land, determined by a third-party appraisal conducted under the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice. Permit-holder improvements are excluded from the appraised value, while anticipated future federal revenues (such as water service contracts and aid to irrigation) are included. Payments are made available to the Secretary, subject to appropriation, for operating the two reservoirs. Counties must also pay survey and administrative costs in advance.

Counties must manage the land for substantially its current uses or for recreation, public access, fish and wildlife habitat, or preservation of natural character. Reconveyance is generally prohibited, except at no cost to a Nebraska public or governmental entity meeting specified conditions.

Conveyances are subject to existing rights, reservoir operational requirements, flowage easements, mineral rights, existing permits, and deed restrictions barring new permanent structures below specified elevations. The United States is held harmless from various liabilities, and temporary flooding is not deemed a taking.

Existing management agreements remain in force until conveyance or three years after enactment. If no agreement is reached within three years, the Secretary continues managing the unaffected land under applicable law. Approved December 23, 2024.

accurate.why: Accurately describes the conveyance of approximately 77 acres across five parcels to Frontier and Hitchcock Counties, the 3-year negotiation deadline, 2-year code compliance requirement, fair market value payment, reconveyance restrictions, flood liability disclaimer, indemnification requirement, and the fallback provision if no agreement is reached.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims. Total acreage (~77.2 acres) and all other figures are accurate.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the purpose: conveying federal reclamation lands at Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs to the Nebraska counties.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Describes the conveyance from federal to county ownership, the change in land management authority.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes reconveyance restrictions (only to NE public/governmental entity), existing rights, flood liability disclaimer, indemnification requirement, and land must be conveyed whole.

effective_dates.why: Notes 3-year negotiation deadline, 2-year code compliance deadline, and Dec 23, 2024 approval date.

major_provisions_covered.why: All key operative provisions covered including the five parcels, payment terms, management requirements, existing agreements, and fallback.

correct_entities.why: Bureau of Reclamation, Secretary of Interior, Hitchcock County, Frontier County, Nebraska, Swanson Reservoir, Hugh Butler Reservoir, John D. Dingell Jr. Act all correctly referenced.

correct_figures.why: ~77 acres total, 3-year deadline, 2-year compliance, Dec 23, 2024 all accurate.

The Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act directs the Secretary of the Interior, through the Bureau of Reclamation, to pursue conveyance of specified federal lands at Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir in Nebraska to Hitchcock County and Frontier County.

The Act covers concession and cabin lands at Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir, including Lakeview Lodge concession land, Red Willow cabin and concession land, and Swanson cabin and concession land. Within three years of enactment, the Secretary must make good faith efforts to negotiate and enter into title transfer agreements with the counties. These agreements must require the land to be conveyed as a whole and not subdivided, and must include plans showing the county’s technical and financial ability to permanently operate and maintain the land. The counties must address applicable state fire, safety, and health code issues within two years after conveyance.

The conveyances must follow the criteria, terms, and conditions of the title transfer provisions of the John D. Dingell, Jr. Conservation, Management, and Recreation Act, notwithstanding one specified eligibility provision. Each county must pay fair market value, determined by an approved third-party appraisal under professional appraisal standards. Improvements made by permit holders are excluded from the appraised value, and the appraisal must consider future federal revenue streams. The counties must also pay survey and administrative transfer costs in advance. Payments are available to the Secretary, subject to appropriation, for activities related to operating the reservoirs.

After conveyance, the counties must manage the land for substantially the same current uses or for recreation, public access, fish and wildlife habitat, or preservation of natural character. Subsequent reconveyance is generally barred, except to certain Nebraska public or governmental entities under specified conditions, including continued public access.

Conveyances remain subject to existing rights, Pick-Sloan operational requirements, flowage easements, management agreement reservations, mineral rights, existing permits and rights-of-way, specified building restrictions below reservoir elevations, and access easements. Existing management agreements and permits remain in effect until the later of three years after enactment or conveyance. If no title transfer agreement is reached within three years, the Secretary continues managing the land under applicable law. The Act also limits federal liability, requires indemnification of the United States, and states that temporary flooding or flood damage is not a federal taking.

The Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act directs the Secretary of the Interior to convey certain federal lands at the Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir to Hitchcock County and Frontier County, Nebraska. 

Under the Act, the Secretary must make good faith efforts to negotiate and enter into title transfer agreements with the counties within three years of the bill's December 23, 2024 enactment. As a condition of conveyance, the lands must be transferred in whole, cannot be subdivided, and must be managed for their current uses or for public recreation, public access, wildlife habitat, or natural preservation. The counties must pay fair market value for the land—excluding any improvements made by permit holders—and cover all administrative and survey costs. Additionally, the counties must demonstrate the capability to manage the land and comply with state safety and health codes within two years of the transfer.

The counties are generally prohibited from reconveying the land, except to a Nebraska public or governmental entity at no cost, provided specific conditions are met. All conveyances remain subject to existing rights, reservoir operations, flowage easements, and deed restrictions that prohibit constructing new permanent structures below specified elevations (2,785 feet at Swanson Reservoir and 2,628 feet at Hugh Butler Reservoir). The United States is exempt from liability for flood damage, and the counties must indemnify the federal government.

During the transition, existing management agreements and permits remain in effect. If a county fails to enter into a title transfer agreement within the three-year deadline, the Secretary will continue to manage the respective lands under applicable law.

The Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act directs the Secretary of the Interior to negotiate the transfer of certain federal lands at Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir in Nebraska to Hitchcock County and Frontier County, respectively. crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act
This bill provides for the conveyance of certain federal lands located at the Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir in Nebraska to Hitchcock County and Frontier County, respectively. The counties must manage the land for substantially the same purposes for which they are being used or for (1) certain public recreation purposes, (2) public access, (3) fish and wildlife habitat, or (4) the preservation of the natural character of the land.
Within three years, the Bureau of Reclamation must enter into negotiations to transfer (1) the Swanson Permitted Cabin Land, the Swanson Permitted Concession Land, and the Lakeview Lodge Permitted Concession Land to Hitchcock County; and (2) the Red Willow Permitted Cabin Land and the Red Willow Permitted Concession Land to Frontier County. Reclamation must offer to convey the land as soon as practicable after the title transfer agreement is made. The land must be conveyed in whole and not subdivided. Additionally, the bill subjects the conveyances to certain existing rights and requirements. Further, the bill limits future conveyances of the land.
Reclamation must enter into a memorandum of agreement with each county to establish and define the roles and responsibilities for actions required to convey the land.
The counties must provide compensation to the Reclamation Fund in an amount equal to the fair market value of the land. The counties must also pay the conveyance costs in advance of the conveyance.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
# Summary of H.R. 8580 — Military Construction, Veterans Affairs, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025

## Purpose
This bill provides federal funding for military construction, the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), and related agencies for fiscal year 2025 (ending September 30, 2025). It is an annual appropriations bill. The text shown passed the House on June 5, 2024, and was placed on the Senate calendar on September 12, 2024.

## Major Provisions

**Title I — Military Construction (Department of Defense):** Appropriates funds for construction and family housing across the Army, Navy/Marine Corps, Air Force, Defense-Wide activities, the National Guard, and Reserve components, plus the NATO Security Investment Program and the Base Closure Account. It includes additional amounts for laboratory facilities, child development centers, barracks, demolition, natural-disaster repairs, and unfunded-priority projects. Administrative provisions impose conditions such as American steel requirements, restrictions on foreign contractors, and congressional notification requirements.

**Title II — Department of Veterans Affairs:** Funds veterans benefits (compensation, pensions, readjustment, insurance, housing and other loan programs), the Veterans Health Administration (medical services, community care, support, facilities, and research), the National Cemetery Administration, departmental administration, information technology, the electronic health record system, the Office of Inspector General, construction, state facility/cemetery grants, and the Cost of War Toxic Exposures Fund. Many health accounts are funded as advance appropriations becoming available October 1, 2025. Provisions establish transfer authorities, reporting requirements, and policy conditions.

**Title III — Related Agencies:** Funds the American Battle Monuments Commission, U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims, Arlington National Cemetery, and the Armed Forces Retirement Home.

**Title IV — General Provisions:** Establishes government-wide spending restrictions and policy riders.

## Notable Conditions and Riders
The bill prohibits funds for: gender-affirming surgical or hormone therapy; most abortions (with exceptions for rape, incest, or life endangerment); diversity, equity, and inclusion training; "Critical Race Theory"; enforcing certain COVID-19 vaccine and mask mandates; and implementing specified executive orders on climate and equity. It restricts purchases of computer equipment tied to China, conditions release of 25% of electronic-health-record funds on certifications, and limits actions affecting firearm background-check reporting and state marijuana program participation.

## Effective Dates
Funds generally cover fiscal year 2025; certain VA medical accounts become available October 1, 2025, and various construction funds remain available through September 30, 2029.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
This bill provides fiscal year 2025 appropriations for military construction, Department of Veterans Affairs programs, and related agencies, with some funds available for multiple years or as advance appropriations for fiscal year 2026.

For the Department of Defense, it funds military construction for the Army, Navy and Marine Corps, Air Force, defense-wide agencies, National Guard and reserve components, the NATO Security Investment Program, base closure activities, and military family and unaccompanied housing. Most military construction funds remain available through September 30, 2029. It sets limits and notification requirements for planning and design funds, transfers, overseas installations, land purchases, certain foreign contracts, and maintenance of general and flag officer housing. It provides additional funds for unfunded priority projects, laboratory facilities, child development centers, barracks planning, demolition, and Air Force natural disaster expenses, generally requiring expenditure plans within 60 days. It prohibits using funds to close or realign Naval Station Guantanamo Bay.

For the Department of Veterans Affairs, the bill funds veterans’ compensation, pensions, readjustment benefits, insurance, housing loans, operating expenses, medical care, community care, medical facilities, research, the National Cemetery Administration, information technology, the electronic health record system, construction, grants for state veterans facilities and cemeteries, and the Cost of War Toxic Exposures Fund. It provides advance fiscal year 2026 funding for several VA benefit and medical accounts. It conditions 25 percent of electronic health record funds on reports and certifications before July 1, 2025, and requires quarterly reporting on finances, benefits performance, and other matters.

The bill includes numerous VA administrative provisions and funding restrictions. It authorizes certain fertility treatment and adoption reimbursement for covered veterans, restricts abortion funding except for rape, incest, or life endangerment, bars funds for gender-affirming surgical procedures or hormone therapies, limits certain flag displays, restricts use of Social Security numbers for authentication, sets requirements for animal research and its elimination by March 9, 2026, and requires construction of the Bakersfield, California outpatient clinic by September 30, 2025 unless barred by court order.

It also funds related agencies, including the American Battle Monuments Commission, the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims, Arlington National Cemetery, and the Armed Forces Retirement Home, and includes government-wide restrictions on transfers, lobbying, pornography on computer networks, certain executive orders, diversity-related activities, and specified procurement.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
H.R. 8580, the Military Construction, Veterans Affairs, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025, provides funding for military construction, the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), and related agencies for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. 

The bill allocates funds for military construction, family housing, and base closures across all military branches, the National Guard, Reserves, and the NATO Security Investment Program, with most construction funds remaining available until September 30, 2029. It prohibits using funds to close or realign the Naval Station at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, or to construct or renovate facilities in the United States to house Guantanamo detainees.

For the VA, the bill funds medical services, prosthetic research, information technology, and veterans' benefits, including advance appropriations for fiscal year 2026. It mandates that the VA prioritize medical care for veterans with service-connected disabilities, lower incomes, or special needs. 

Additionally, the bill places several restrictions on the use of funds, including:
- Prohibiting funding for abortions, except in cases of rape, incest, or life-endangering physical conditions, and blocking the implementation of the VA's September 9, 2022 interim final rule on abortion.
- Banning funds for gender-affirming surgical procedures or hormone therapies.
- Prohibiting the enforcement of COVID-19 vaccine mandates for VA healthcare personnel.
- Requiring the VA to implement a plan to eliminate research on canines, felines, or non-human primates by March 9, 2026, with strict interim approval and reporting conditions.
- Preventing the VA from reporting mentally incapacitated veterans to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System without a judicial finding that they pose a danger.
- Prohibiting funds for diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) training, Critical Race Theory, and several executive orders related to climate change and environmental justice.
- Restricting the procurement of office IT equipment from entities with Chinese ownership or ties to the Chinese military.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
H.R. 8580, the Military Construction, Veterans Affairs, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025, provides funding for the Department of Defense (DOD), the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), and related agencies for fiscal year 2025. 

For the DOD, the bill appropriates billions for military construction projects across the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Air Force, National Guard, and Reserve components, as well as for family housing and the NATO Security Investment Program. It prohibits funds from being used to close or realign the U.S. Naval Station at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, or to construct facilities in the U.S. to house detainees transferred from there.

For the VA, the legislation funds veterans' benefits, including compensation, pensions, and readjustment benefits. It also provides substantial funding for the Veterans Health Administration for medical services, community care, facilities, and research. The bill includes provisions restricting VA research using canines, felines, and non-human primates, requiring the phase-out of such research by March 9, 2026. It prohibits the use of funds for surgical procedures or hormone therapies for gender-affirming care and restricts funding for abortions, except in cases of rape, incest, or when the life of the mother is endangered. The bill also funds the implementation of the VA's electronic health record system, contingent on the Secretary certifying system stability and readiness.

Title III funds related agencies, including the American Battle Monuments Commission, the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims, Arlington National Cemetery, and the Armed Forces Retirement Home.

Title IV contains general provisions applying to all funds in the Act. It prohibits the use of funds for diversity, equity, and inclusion training, Critical Race Theory, COVID-19 mask mandates, and the implementation of several executive orders related to climate change and environmental justice. It also bars funds from being used to take discriminatory action against individuals based on sincerely held religious beliefs regarding marriage. Funds are generally available for obligation through the end of fiscal year 2025, with some accounts available through 2026, 2029, or until expended.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill provides appropriations for military construction, the Department of Veterans Affairs, and related agencies for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. Title I funds military construction projects for the Army, Navy, Air Force, defense-wide activities, National Guard, Reserves, NATO, base closure, and family housing. Most construction funds remain available through September 30, 2029. It sets limits on planning and design spending, requires congressional notification if those limits are exceeded, and includes additional amounts for unfunded priority projects subject to authorization and an expenditure plan. Administrative provisions restrict cost-plus contracts, land purchases, and new bases without specific appropriations; require preference for U.S. firms on certain overseas contracts; and prohibit the closure or realignment of Naval Station Guantanamo Bay, Cuba.

Title II appropriates funds for veterans’ benefits, medical services (including community care, support, facilities, and research), the electronic health record, national cemeteries, departmental administration, major and minor construction, grants for state extended care facilities and veterans cemeteries, and the Cost of War Toxic Exposures Fund. Many medical care funds become available on October 1, 2025, with some extended availability. The title includes transfer authorities and reporting requirements. It imposes several policy restrictions: funds may not be used for abortion except in cases of rape, incest, or to save the life of the mother; for gender-affirming care; to enforce certain COVID-19 vaccination mandates; to display flags other than specified ones; or to provide services to individuals unlawfully present who are ineligible for VA care. It also prohibits reporting veterans as mentally defective to the gun background check system without a judicial finding of dangerousness.

Title III funds the American Battle Monuments Commission, the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims, Arlington National Cemetery, and the Armed Forces Retirement Home. Title IV contains government-wide provisions, including prohibitions on using funds for diversity, equity, and inclusion training, critical race theory, certain climate-related executive orders, and procurement from entities with Chinese ownership ties. It also bans construction or expansion of U.S. facilities to house Guantanamo detainees and restricts first-class travel. The act may be cited as the Military Construction, Veterans Affairs, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025.

bills.title: DETECT Fentanyl and Xylazine Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The DETECT Fentanyl and Xylazine Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-186) directs the Science and Technology Directorate of the Department of Homeland Security to develop improved capabilities for detecting and identifying illicit drugs, including those present in very low concentrations, such as fentanyl and xylazine.

The bill amends Section 302 of the Homeland Security Act of 2002 by adding a new responsibility. In coordination with the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA), the Directorate must conduct research, development, testing, evaluation, and cost-benefit analyses to improve the safety, effectiveness, and efficiency of detection equipment and the reference libraries used by federal, state, local, Tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies. This work specifically includes developing: (1) portable equipment capable of detecting and identifying drugs with minimal or no handling of the sample; (2) equipment that can separate complex mixtures containing low drug concentrations and high concentrations of cutting agents to enable field identification; and (3) technologies using machine learning or artificial intelligence (as defined in the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020) to predict whether substances in a sample are controlled substance analogues or new psychoactive substances not yet listed in existing reference libraries.

In carrying out these activities, the Under Secretary for Science and Technology must follow the recommendations and best practices in the National Institute of Standards and Technology's Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (NIST AI 100-1) or any successor document. The Under Secretary must also set the Directorate's research and evaluation priorities based on the latest available information, including drugs identified as threats in the most recent Homeland Threat Assessment, the DEA's State and Territory Report on Enduring and Emerging Threats, or successor documents.

The Act includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it limits the authority of agencies already managing or involved in drug equipment and reference libraries. The law was approved on December 23, 2024.

The Act adds a new responsibility for the Directorate to carry out, in coordination with the Drug Enforcement Administration, research, development, testing, evaluation, and cost-benefit analyses aimed at improving the safety, effectiveness, and efficiency of drug-detection equipment and reference libraries used by Federal, State, local, Tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies. The covered technologies include portable equipment that can detect and identify drugs with minimal or no handling of samples; equipment that can separate complex mixtures containing low concentrations of drugs and high concentrations of cutting agents into component parts for field identification and detection; and technologies using machine learning, artificial intelligence, or other techniques to predict whether substances are controlled substance analogues or other new psychoactive substances not yet included in available reference libraries.

bills.title: Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This bill, the "Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2025" (H.R. 8752), provides funding for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. It passed the House of Representatives on June 28, 2024.

**Appropriations by area.** The bill funds DHS operations across five titles:
- **Departmental management and oversight** (Title I), including the Office of the Secretary, the Management Directorate, intelligence and situational awareness, and the Inspector General.
- **Security, enforcement, and investigations** (Title II), funding Customs and Border Protection (CBP, roughly $16.6 billion in operations), Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE, roughly $10.5 billion), the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, and the Secret Service.
- **Protection and preparedness** (Title III), funding the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, FEMA grants, the Disaster Relief Fund ($22.7 billion), and the National Flood Insurance Fund.
- **Research, training, and services** (Title IV), funding citizenship services (E-Verify), training centers, the Science and Technology Directorate, and weapons-of-mass-destruction programs.

**Major policy provisions.** The bill sets specific conditions on spending, including:
- Requiring ICE to maintain at least 50,000 detention beds and enroll non-detained aliens in GPS monitoring.
- Allocating $600 million for border physical barriers (steel bollard barriers 18–30 feet high) and prohibiting funds to dismantle existing barriers.
- Prohibiting use of the CBP One app to facilitate parole, barring new border-crossing fees, and blocking certain enforcement-priority memos and asylum rules.
- Restricting funds for gender-affirming care in ICE custody and most ICE-funded abortions (with exceptions for rape, incest, or life endangerment).
- Prohibiting funding for diversity/equity executive orders, certain climate executive orders, a "Disinformation Governance Board," and electric-vehicle purchases.
- Reducing one named employee's salary to $1 and barring funding for Secretary Mayorkas's salary.
- Requiring extensive reporting, reprogramming notifications, and oversight access for Members of Congress to detention facilities.

**Rescissions.** The bill rescinds $600 million in prior border-barrier construction funds and $154 million from the Nonrecurring Expenses Fund.

Various provisions carry specific effective dates (e.g., contraceptive coverage provisions effective 30 and 180 days after enactment) and deadlines for reports and expenditure plans.

This bill provides fiscal year 2025 appropriations for the Department of Homeland Security and its components through September 30, 2025, with some funds available for later years or until expended. It funds departmental management, intelligence, the Inspector General, U.S. Customs and Border Protection, Immigration and Customs Enforcement, the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, the Secret Service, the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, the Federal Emergency Management Agency, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services, Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers, the Science and Technology Directorate, and the Countering Weapons of Mass Destruction Office.

Major funding includes operations, procurement, construction, research, and assistance accounts; $22.741 billion for FEMA’s Disaster Relief Fund for major disasters; FEMA homeland security, nonprofit security, fire, emergency management, flood mapping, emergency food and shelter, and community project grants; and funding for the E-Verify program. The bill directs $600 million of CBP procurement funds for physical barriers and $300 million for border security technologies, restricts barrier funds to specified steel bollard pedestrian barriers, requires barrier obligations within 120 days, and bars use of funds to dismantle existing U.S.-Mexico barriers unless repaired or replaced.

The bill sets numerous conditions and prohibitions. It bars new land border crossing fees and related studies; prohibits use of CBP One or a successor application to facilitate parole; restricts certain student visa admissions, parole for certain Chinese nationals into the Northern Mariana Islands, and admission of certain foreign-manufactured aerosol unmanned aircraft systems. For ICE, it requires at least 50,000 detention beds, funds detention and removal operations, supports 287(g) agreements, restricts certain immigration enforcement policies, limits transport of certain aliens into the interior except for immigration enforcement, and restricts abortion funding with exceptions for life endangerment, rape, or incest.

The bill imposes reporting, notification, acquisition oversight, pilot program, grant, reprogramming, and transfer requirements. It includes Coast Guard TRICARE contraceptive cost-sharing and supply provisions effective 30 and 180 days after enactment, respectively. General provisions restrict use of funds for specified executive orders, rules, programs, electric vehicles, COVID-19 mask mandates, a DHS environmental justice strategy, Safe Mobility Offices, certain grants to jurisdictions limiting immigration enforcement cooperation, and salaries or expenses of specified persons or positions. It rescinds specified prior DHS unobligated balances, including $600 million for southwest border barrier construction and $154 million from the DHS Nonrecurring Expenses Fund.

For security and enforcement, it funds U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, and the Secret Service. Notably, it mandates maintaining at least 50,000 ICE detention beds, requires physical barrier construction ($600 million) to begin within 120 days of enactment, and requires mandatory GPS monitoring for all individuals on ICE's non-detained docket. For preparedness and recovery, it funds the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency and the Federal Emergency Management Agency, including $22.74 billion for the Disaster Relief Fund. It also funds USCIS E-Verify operations, the Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers, and the Science and Technology Directorate.

The bill introduces significant policy restrictions and changes to existing operations. It prohibits funds from being used to dismantle existing border barriers, utilize the CBP One application for alien parole, or implement specific civil immigration enforcement guidelines. It also blocks funding for abortions (except in cases of rape, incest, or threat to the mother's life), gender-affirming care for ICE detainees, the DHS Equity Action Plan, several climate-related Executive Orders, and a Disinformation Governance Board. Additionally, it defunds the salary of the DHS Secretary and reduces the salary of a specific USCIS officer to $1.

Material conditions and effective dates include prohibiting cost-sharing for prescription contraceptives for Coast Guard members and dependents (effective 30 days after enactment) and mandating 365-day contraceptive supply coverage (effective 180 days after enactment). Finally, the bill rescinds $600 million in prior border barrier funding and $154 million from the DHS Nonrecurring Expenses Fund.

z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
H.R. 8752, the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2025, provides funding for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and its components for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. The bill allocates specific amounts to agencies including U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the Coast Guard, the Secret Service, the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA).

The bill includes numerous policy provisions that restrict or direct how funds may be used. It prohibits funding for several current immigration policies, such as the "Circumvention of Lawful Pathways" and "Public Charge" rules, and bars funds from being used to parole aliens via the CBP One app. It requires ICE to maintain at least 50,000 detention beds and mandates GPS monitoring for all aliens on the non-detained docket. Additionally, the bill restricts funding for electric vehicles, COVID-19 mask mandates, certain climate change executive orders, and diversity, equity, and inclusion programs. It reduces the salary of a specific USCIS officer to $1 and prohibits any funds from being used to pay the salary and expenses of the Secretary of Homeland Security. The legislation also includes reporting requirements, mandating that DHS submit various expenditure plans, acquisition briefings, and estimates of anticipated border arrivals to congressional committees. Certain funds are made available for multiple fiscal years, and the bill rescinds $600 million from prior CBP barrier construction funds and $154 million from the DHS Nonrecurring Expenses Fund.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill provides appropriations for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. It allocates specific funding amounts across DHS components, including Customs and Border Protection (CBP), Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, the Secret Service, the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, and the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA).

Beyond setting funding levels, the bill includes numerous policy riders and restrictions. Key provisions direct funding for border security, including $600 million for the acquisition and deployment of steel bollard pedestrian physical barriers along the southwest border, consistent with a 2020 Border Security Improvement Plan. It mandates that ICE maintain a level of not less than 50,000 detention beds and requires all non-detained aliens to be enrolled in the Alternatives to Detention Program with mandatory GPS monitoring.

The legislation prohibits the use of funds for several specific immigration-related activities, such as using the CBP One application to facilitate parole, implementing certain prior enforcement guideline memoranda, and paroling nationals of the People's Republic of China into the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands under a specific program. It also restricts funding for gender-affirming care for individuals in ICE custody, except under narrow circumstances, and prohibits funds for abortions for ICE detainees except in cases of rape, incest, or to save the life of the mother.

Several administrative provisions are established, including requirements for congressional notification before certain fund transfers, grant awards, or reprogramming actions. The bill also rescinds $600 million from prior-year CBP border barrier construction funds and $154 million from the DHS Nonrecurring Expenses Fund, among other rescissions. It imposes various other policy prohibitions, such as barring funds for a Disinformation Governance Board, for enforcing certain COVID-19 mask mandates, and for implementing specific executive orders related to climate and equity.

bills.title: Legislative Branch Appropriations Act, 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This bill, the "Legislative Branch Appropriations Act, 2025," provides funding for the operations of the Legislative Branch of the federal government for fiscal year 2025 (ending September 30, 2025).

**Major Appropriations.** The bill funds the House of Representatives (approximately $1.93 billion for salaries and expenses, including Members' representational allowances, committees, and intern compensation), the Capitol Police (over $830 million), the Architect of the Capitol (covering the Capitol building, grounds, office buildings, and power plant), the Library of Congress (including the Copyright Office and Congressional Research Service), the Government Publishing Office, the Government Accountability Office (about $896.7 million), the Congressional Budget Office, and several smaller offices and joint committees. It also provides $174,000 to the widow of the late Representative Donald M. Payne, Jr.

**Notable Provisions and Changes to Existing Law:**
- Unspent Members' representational allowances must be returned to the Treasury for deficit or debt reduction (Sec. 110).
- Vehicle leasing from Member allowances is capped at $1,000 per month per vehicle (Sec. 111).
- Capitol Police training outside the U.S. requires prior approval from the Capitol Police Board (Sec. 115).
- Funds may not be used to purchase drones made in or affiliated with the People's Republic of China, except for national security.
- The bill amends laws on Energy Policy Act lease requirements (Sec. 113), the child care center fund (Sec. 114), and fiduciary relationships for Members providing medical or dental care (Sec. 211).
- Section 209 prohibits procurement of certain telecommunications and surveillance equipment from named Chinese companies and foreign adversaries, with phased effective dates and a limited waiver authority.
- Section 212 bars funding for diversity, equity, and inclusion training promoting specified "divisive concepts" related to race or sex.
- Section 213 prohibits discriminatory action against persons holding the belief that marriage is between one man and one woman.
- Section 214 blocks cost-of-living pay adjustments for Members of Congress in fiscal year 2025.

The bill includes general provisions on transfers, consulting contracts, Capitol tours, and computer network content. It was reported by the House Committee on Appropriations on June 17, 2024.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Legislative Branch Appropriations Act, 2025, provides funding for the operations of the United States Congress and legislative branch agencies for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. The bill appropriates funds for the House of Representatives, Capitol Police, Congressional Budget Office, Architect of the Capitol, Library of Congress, Government Publishing Office, and Government Accountability Office.

The bill introduces several administrative changes to existing law. It requires unused Members' representational allowances to be deposited in the Treasury for deficit or debt reduction, limits Member vehicle leases to $1,000 per month, and prohibits cost-of-living adjustments for Members of Congress during fiscal year 2025. Additionally, it amends existing law to allow the House Child Care Center revolving fund to cover telecommunications expenses, requires Capitol Police Board approval for international training of Capitol Police employees, and exempts the Director of the Government Publishing Office and the Librarian of Congress from statutory pay freezes. It also amends Title 5 to clarify that a Member providing direct medical or dental care does not establish a fiduciary relationship.

The bill establishes several funding restrictions and conditions. It prohibits contractor bonuses for projects that are behind schedule or over budget, unless deviations are due to unforeseeable events or government-driven changes. It blocks funds for computer networks that do not restrict pornography, except for law enforcement or official government activities. Furthermore, the bill bans the procurement of telecommunications or video surveillance equipment from specified Chinese entities (such as Huawei and ZTE) or foreign adversaries, effective in fiscal year 2025, with waiver options up to two years. Finally, it prohibits funding for diversity, equity, and inclusion training that promotes divisive concepts related to race or sex, and prevents the federal government from taking discriminatory actions against persons acting on a sincerely held religious belief or moral conviction that marriage is a union of one man and one woman.

Payne, Jr. Unspent Members’ Representational Allowance funds must be returned to the Treasury for deficit reduction or debt reduction. bills.title: FAFSA Deadline Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The FAFSA Deadline Act (Public Law 118-145) amends the Higher Education Act of 1965 to require that the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) be made available earlier in the application cycle.

Under prior law, Section 483(d)(4) of the Higher Education Act directed the Secretary of Education to make the FAFSA available no later than January 1 of the applicant's planned year of enrollment, and "to the maximum extent practicable," on or around October 1 of the year before enrollment. The Act replaces this language with a firm requirement that the application be made available no later than October 1 prior to the applicant's planned year of enrollment.

The Act also adds new certification and testimony requirements tied to each application processing cycle. Before each cycle begins, the Secretary must, no later than September 1, certify to the authorizing committees of Congress either that the Department of Education will meet the October 1 deadline, or that it will not meet that deadline. If the Secretary certifies that the deadline will not be met, the Secretary must, no later than September 30, testify before the authorizing committees regarding the anticipated failure to meet the deadline and the financial impact that failure will have on students and families.

The Act was approved on December 11, 2024.

The FAFSA Deadline Act (Public Law 118-145) establishes an earlier, fixed deadline for the annual Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) application processing cycle and introduces new congressional oversight requirements for the Department of Education. 

Specifically, the Act amends the Higher Education Act of 1965 by changing the start date of the FAFSA application processing cycle. Under previous law, the cycle was required to begin no later than January 1 of the applicant's planned year of enrollment, with a goal of starting on or around October 1 to the maximum extent practicable. The Act replaces this flexible timeline with a strict requirement that the processing cycle must begin not later than October 1 prior to the applicant's planned year of enrollment.

Additionally, the Act establishes certification and testimony requirements for the Secretary of Education prior to the start of each processing cycle. No later than September 1 of each year, the Secretary must certify to the authorizing congressional committees whether the Department of Education will or will not meet the October 1 deadline. If the Secretary certifies that the Department will fail to meet the deadline, the Secretary must testify before the authorizing committees no later than September 30. This testimony must address the anticipated failure to meet the deadline as well as the expected financial impact of the delay on students and families.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The FAFSA Deadline Act amends the Higher Education Act of 1965 to create a firm annual deadline for making the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) available. Under prior law, the Department of Education was required to make the FAFSA available no later than January 1 of the applicant’s planned enrollment year, with a goal of releasing it on or around the preceding October 1 “to the maximum extent practicable.” This Act removes that flexibility and requires that the FAFSA be made available by October 1 prior to the applicant’s planned enrollment year, with no later fallback.

The Act also imposes new accountability and transparency measures on the Secretary of Education for each application processing cycle. No later than September 1, before the cycle begins, the Secretary must certify to the congressional authorizing committees either that the Department will meet the October 1 deadline or that it will not. If the certification states the deadline will not be met, the Secretary must testify before those committees no later than September 30. That testimony must address the anticipated failure to meet the deadline and describe the financial impact it will have on students and families.

The law contains no exceptions and took effect upon its approval on December 11, 2024.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
FAFSA Deadline Act
This act requires the Department of Education (ED) to make the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) available by October 1 of each year. (Students apply for federal student aid by completing the FAFSA.)
Under current law, ED must enable applicants to submit the and initiate the processing of submitted by January 1 of the applicant's planned year of enrollment, and to the maximum extent practicable, on or around October 1 prior to the applicant's planned year of enrollment. This act removes the January 1 flexibility and imposes a statutory deadline for release of the as October 1.
The act also requires ED to certify to Congress by September 1 whether or not the FAFSA will be ready on October 1. If the FAFSA will not be ready by October 1, then the act requires the Secretary of Education to testify to Congress on (1) the anticipated failure to meet the deadline, and (2) the financial impact on students and families.

bills.title: Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This bill, the "Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025," provides funding for energy and water programs for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. It is organized into five titles.

**Title I – Corps of Engineers (Civil):** Appropriates funds to the Army Corps of Engineers, including $159 million for investigations, $3.01 billion for construction, $370 million for Mississippi River and tributaries work, $5.714 billion for operation and maintenance, $218 million for the regulatory program, and $200 million for cleanup of early atomic-energy sites. General provisions set reprogramming rules and bar use of funds for certain activities (e.g., restricting firearm regulations at water projects, limiting open-lake disposal of dredged material from Lake Erie).

**Title II – Department of the Interior:** Funds the Central Utah Project ($23 million) and the Bureau of Reclamation, including $1.773 billion for Water and Related Resources and $33 million for California Bay-Delta restoration. It extends and increases authorizations for certain drought-relief and water programs and directs operation of the Central Valley and State Water Projects per specified 2019 biological opinions.

**Title III – Department of Energy:** Provides funding across DOE programs, including $1.96 billion for Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, $8.39 billion for Science, $20.34 billion for Weapons Activities, $2.445 billion for Defense Nuclear Nonproliferation, and $7.132 billion for Defense Environmental Cleanup. It amends the Natural Gas Act to give FERC exclusive authority over LNG export/import facilities and deem such trade in the public interest. It transfers prior-year balances toward nuclear reactor demonstration projects and bars funds for specified DOE energy-efficiency rules and equity initiatives.

**Title IV – Independent Agencies:** Funds regional commissions, the Defense Nuclear Facilities Safety Board, and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission ($955 million), among others.

**Title V – General Provisions:** Imposes government-wide restrictions, including bans on funds for Critical Race Theory, certain diversity/equity executive orders, COVID-19 mask/vaccine mandates, and the Wuhan Institute of Virology.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
This bill provides fiscal year 2025 appropriations for energy and water development and related agencies through September 30, 2025, with many funds remaining available until expended or through September 30, 2026. It funds the Army Corps of Engineers civil works programs, including investigations, construction, Mississippi River and tributaries, operation and maintenance, regulatory activities, cleanup of formerly used atomic energy sites, and flood and coastal emergencies. It funds the Department of the Interior’s Central Utah Project and Bureau of Reclamation water programs, including Central Valley Project restoration and California Bay-Delta restoration.

The bill funds Department of Energy programs for energy efficiency and renewable energy, cybersecurity and energy security, electricity, grid deployment, nuclear energy, fossil energy and carbon management, petroleum reserves, the Energy Information Administration, environmental cleanup, science, nuclear waste disposal, technology transitions, ARPA-E, loan program administration, Indian energy, departmental administration, and the Inspector General. It also funds the National Nuclear Security Administration’s weapons, nonproliferation, naval reactors, and federal salaries accounts; defense environmental cleanup and other defense activities; power marketing administrations; the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission; regional commissions; the Nuclear Regulatory Commission; the Defense Nuclear Facilities Safety Board; and the Nuclear Waste Technical Review Board.

The bill sets reprogramming, transfer, notification, reporting, and work plan requirements for funded agencies. It restricts certain uses of funds, including for specified Corps and Reclamation activities, certain DOE rules and initiatives, Strategic Petroleum Reserve sales involving China, awards to “entities of concern,” certain procurements involving Chinese ownership, COVID-19 mask or vaccine mandates, Critical Race Theory-related activities, specified equity executive orders, and certain energy conservation rules. It requires guidance documents on the 2023 “waters of the United States” rule within 15 days and a Corps baseline report within 60 days.

The bill amends existing law by extending and increasing authorizations related to CALFED, drought relief, water recycling and desalination, modifying WIIN Act consultation provisions, changing Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act eligibility language, and amending the Natural Gas Act to give FERC exclusive authority over approvals for natural gas import and export facilities while preserving presidential sanctions and emergency authorities. It also transfers specified unobligated balances to nuclear energy projects and reduces certain Inflation Reduction Act funding amounts.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
H.R. 8997, the Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025, provides fiscal year 2025 funding for federal water resources, energy research, and national security programs. 

For civil works, the bill funds the Army Corps of Engineers, including $3.01 billion for construction and $5.714 billion for operations and maintenance. It prohibits the Corps from enforcing regulations that ban firearms at water resources development projects if possession complies with state law. Within the Department of the Interior, the bill allocates $1.773 billion to the Bureau of Reclamation, extends authorizations under the Calfed Bay-Delta Authorization Act and the Reclamation States Emergency Drought Relief Act through 2025, and mandates operating the Central Valley and California State Water Projects under specific 2019 biological opinions.

The Department of Energy receives funding for energy programs, nuclear security, and environmental cleanup, including $20.338 billion for atomic weapons activities and $8.39 billion for science. The bill amends the Natural Gas Act to grant the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission exclusive authority over natural gas export and import facilities. It also bars Strategic Petroleum Reserve sales to Chinese Communist Party-controlled entities, blocks funding for the Justice40 initiative, and transfers certain unobligated balances to nuclear energy programs. Additionally, the bill funds independent agencies, including $955.3 million for the Nuclear Regulatory Commission.

General provisions prohibit funding for COVID-19 mask or vaccine mandates, the Wuhan Institute of Virology, promoting Critical Race Theory, implementing specific federal equity executive orders, or taking discriminatory actions against individuals based on religious beliefs regarding marriage. It also blocks the finalization or enforcement of several Department of Energy conservation standards.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
This bill, the Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2025, provides funding for federal energy and water programs for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025. 

Title I appropriates funds for the Department of the Army Corps of Engineers–Civil, covering investigations, construction, operation and maintenance, and emergency flood control. It establishes reprogramming limits and prohibits the use of funds for certain dredged material placements in Lake Erie and water supply reallocation studies at Wolf Creek Dam. It also prohibits regulations banning firearms at water resources development projects if the individual is legally allowed to possess them and complies with state law.

Title II funds the Department of the Interior, including the Bureau of Reclamation and the Central Utah Project. It amends the Calfed Bay-Delta Authorization Act and the Reclamation States Emergency Drought Relief Act, extending authorizations and increasing funding limits. It also mandates specific operations for the Central Valley Project and California State Water Project.

Title III appropriates funds for the Department of Energy, covering energy programs, atomic energy defense activities, and power marketing administrations. It restricts funds from being awarded to entities of concern, prohibits certain procurements from China, and restricts admittance of Russian or Chinese citizens to nuclear weapons production facilities. It amends the Natural Gas Act to grant the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission exclusive authority over LNG terminal approvals, deeming exports in the public interest. It also transfers unobligated balances to support advanced reactor projects.

Title IV funds independent agencies, including the Nuclear Regulatory Commission and various regional commissions. Title V contains general provisions prohibiting the use of funds to influence congressional action, promote Critical Race Theory, implement COVID-19 mask or vaccine mandates, or display flags other than the U.S. flag or POW/MIA flag. It also includes provisions protecting individuals from discrimination based on religious beliefs regarding marriage. Most funds are available until expended or September 30, 2026.
